title: Challenges in school №41
description: There are $$$n$$$ children, who study at the school №41. It is well-known that they are good mathematicians. Once at a break, they arranged a challenge for themselves. All children arranged in a row and turned heads either to the left or to the right. Children can do the following: in one second several pairs of neighboring children who are looking at each other can simultaneously turn the head in the opposite direction. For instance, the one who was looking at the right neighbor turns left and vice versa for the second child. Moreover, every second at least one pair of neighboring children performs such action. They are going to finish when there is no pair of neighboring children who are looking at each other. You are given the number $$$n$$$, the initial arrangement of children and the number $$$k$$$. You have to find a way for the children to act if they want to finish the process in exactly $$$k$$$ seconds. More formally, for each of the $$$k$$$ moves, you need to output the numbers of the children who turn left during this move. For instance, for the configuration shown below and $$$k = 2$$$ children can do the following steps: At the beginning, two pairs make move: $$$(1, 2)$$$ and $$$(3, 4)$$$. After that, we receive the following configuration: At the second move pair $$$(2, 3)$$$ makes the move. The final configuration is reached. Good job. It is guaranteed that if the solution exists, it takes not more than $$$n^2$$$ "headturns".
input_spec: The first line of input contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$k$$$ ($$$2 \le n \le 3000$$$, $$$1 \le k \le 3000000$$$)  — the number of children and required number of moves. The next line contains a string of length $$$n$$$ and consists only of characters L and R , where L means that the child looks to the left and R means that the child looks to the right.
output_spec: If there is no solution, print a single line with number $$$-1$$$. Otherwise, output $$$k$$$ lines. Each line has to start with a number $$$n_i$$$ ($$$1\le n_i \le \frac{n}{2}$$$)  — the number of pairs of children, who turn at this move. After that print $$$n_i$$$ distinct integers  — the numbers of the children who will turn left during this move. After performing all "headturns", there can't be a pair of two neighboring children looking at each other. If there are many solutions, print any of them.

routes.name: Parallel bubble sort with round control
routes.description: Model the process as parallel bubble sort on a binary array (R=1, L=0). Compute the minimum and maximum number of rounds, then construct exactly k rounds by controlling how many swaps to perform per round.

observations.title: The process is bubble sort on a binary sequence
observations.description: Encoding R as 1 and L as 0, an 'RL' pair is a (1,0) inversion. Swapping such a pair is exactly one step of bubble sort. The process terminates when all 0s precede all 1s, i.e., the sequence is sorted.

hints.nudge: What well-known sorting process swaps adjacent out-of-order elements?
hints.question: If you encode L as 0 and R as 1, what does an RL pair look like, and what does swapping it remind you of?
hints.formal: Map R to 1 and L to 0. Each RL pair is an adjacent inversion (1,0). Swapping all such pairs simultaneously is one round of parallel bubble sort. The process ends when the binary array is sorted.

observations.title: Maximum rounds equals the total number of inversions
observations.description: Since we must perform at least one swap per round, the maximum number of rounds is achieved by doing exactly one swap per round. The total number of swaps is fixed: each R must pass every L to its right, so max rounds = total inversions = sum over each R of the count of L's to its right.

hints.nudge: What is the slowest possible pace? How many total individual swaps are needed regardless of grouping?
hints.question: If you do exactly one swap per second, how many seconds does the entire process take? How does that relate to inversion count?
hints.formal: The total number of swaps is the number of inversions: for each R at position i, count L's at positions j > i. Maximum rounds = total inversions, achieved by performing exactly one swap per round.

observations.title: Minimum rounds from parallel execution with dependencies
observations.description: The minimum rounds come from doing all possible non-overlapping swaps simultaneously each round (like parallel bubble sort). For each R, the number of rounds it needs is the count of L's to its right, but consecutive R's create dependencies: each subsequent R must wait one extra round compared to the previous R. Scanning right to left, track a running minimum that enforces this constraint.

hints.nudge: If you greedily do all possible swaps each round, some R's can't all start moving at the same time. Why?
hints.question: Consider two consecutive R's both needing to pass L's to their right. Can they both complete their first swap in the same round? What constraint does this impose on the minimum?
hints.formal: Scan right to left. For each R with c L's to its right, its contribution to minimum rounds is max(c, previous_minimum + 1). The overall minimum is the final value after processing all R's that have L's to their right.

observations.title: k is achievable if and only if min <= k <= max
observations.description: Any value of k between the minimum and maximum number of rounds is achievable. If k is outside this range, output -1. This is because we can always split a round with multiple swaps into two rounds, increasing the count by 1, and conversely merge single-swap rounds if needed.

hints.nudge: If you can do the process in min rounds and in max rounds, what about values in between?
hints.question: If a round has 3 swaps, can you split it into a round with 2 swaps and a round with 1 swap? What does that do to the total round count?
hints.formal: A round with m >= 2 swaps can always be split: do m-1 swaps in one round, 1 in the next. This increases the total by 1. Repeated splitting can reach any k in [min, max]. If k < min or k > max, output -1.

observations.title: Construct the schedule by capping swaps per round
observations.description: To hit exactly k rounds: simulate parallel bubble sort, but in each round limit the number of swaps you perform so that the remaining swaps can fill the remaining rounds. Specifically, in round i, perform at most (remaining_total_swaps - remaining_rounds + 1) swaps. Once remaining swaps equals remaining rounds, switch to doing exactly one swap per round.

hints.nudge: You know the total swaps left and the rounds left. How do you decide how many swaps to do this round?
hints.question: If you have T swaps remaining and R rounds remaining, what is the maximum number of swaps you can do this round while still being able to fill all R rounds?
hints.formal: In each round, find all available RL pairs. Perform min(available_pairs, total_remaining - rounds_remaining + 1) swaps. When total_remaining == rounds_remaining, switch to single-swap mode (one swap per round) for the rest.
